subject: Aaron Swartz — the prodigy who tried to free knowledge, and the disproportionate prosecution he did not survive
central_question: How does a country put a gifted young man who tried to give knowledge away on trial as a thirteen-count felon, when the supposed victim did not want charges — and what does it say about us that, after he was gone, almost nothing about the law actually changed?
viewer_promise: By the end, the viewer understands who Aaron Swartz really was and what he built, exactly what he did at MIT/JSTOR and how the law responded, why the famous '35 years' was a misleading theoretical maximum, and the still-open question of who owns knowledge — told humanely, with care around his death and a quiet support resource.
surprise: The 'victim,' JSTOR, settled civilly and openly did not want him prosecuted; MIT stayed neutral; the realistic plea on the table was about six months, not 35 years; and after his death the reform bill named for him never passed and the law was never meaningfully changed.
stakes: Who controls access to publicly funded knowledge, and how a justice system decides what counts as a crime and what counts as proportional punishment when a sweeping, decades-old computer law meets a young idealist.
target_audience: English-speaking general audience interested in technology, true-crime/justice, the open internet, and how the law actually works
demand_evidence: Aaron Swartz is a widely recognized, advertiser-sensitive-but-coverable figure; the documentary 'The Internet's Own Boy' and the SOPA/PIPA fight keep sustained search interest.

Strong moral-curiosity hook ('a young man charged with thirteen felonies for downloading academic papers a library didn't want to press charges over') with a David-vs-system shape and a genuinely unresolved ending.
competition: Covered by a major documentary and extensive reporting, but most retellings either lionize or skim the legal nuance; few hold the restrained, systemic, record-based line (35-years-as-theoretical-maximum; no single-cause narrative) while still landing the human weight.
differentiation: Feature-length, restrained, record-based, and humane: explains what he actually built and did, refuses the single-villain story, qualifies the 35-year figure honestly, attributes all causation about his death to the family — and turns the unresolved 'who owns knowledge' question into the share/debate engine.
research_feasibility: High: the superseding indictment and DOJ/US Attorney releases, the MIT Abelson Report, JSTOR's own statement, the family statement, Lessig's essay, Orin Kerr's legal analysis, the RSS 1.0 spec, and congressional records for Aaron's Law are all authoritative and public.
visual_feasibility: High (symbolic, no real-person likeness): a glowing locked archive, abstract feeds of light, an archetypal night campus and wiring-closet glow, stacked statute-towers, an empty chair, a door left ajar — all symbolic; 988 resource and all text added in Remotion.
risk_notes: R3 + SENSITIVE: death by suicide. State it ONCE, exactly: 'died by suicide on January 11, 2013, at age 26.' NO method, NO location, NO note, NO scene/reenactment. NEVER 'committed suicide.' 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline on-screen and in description; CTA quiet and respectful.

NO single-cause narrative: do NOT state as fact that the prosecution 'caused' his death. Attribute all such causation to the family's statement. Foreground systemic issues (CFAA breadth, proportionality, institutional neutrality), not one villain.

THE 35 YEARS: always the THEORETICAL stacked statutory maximum, immediately paired with the ~6-month plea signal (per attorney Elliot Peters) and the scholarly critique (Orin Kerr) that the maximum wildly overstates realistic exposure. NEVER show '35 years' unqualified.

Wording locks: co-authored RSS 1.0 as a teenager (NOT invented RSS); KEY COLLABORATOR on Markdown (NOT co-created); helped build the technical layer of Creative Commons (NOT founded); Reddit CO-OWNER via the Infogami merger (co-founder DISPUTED). He was CHARGED, never tried or convicted.

JSTOR settled civilly and did not want criminal charges; MIT stayed neutral. Carmen Ortiz's 'Stealing is stealing' line is the prosecution's attributed position, not the narrator's. 'Aaron's Law' was proposed and NEVER passed; the CFAA was not meaningfully reformed. No real-person likeness in any visual.
Dedicated R3 legal/rights + safe-handling review REQUIRED before publish. Durable source content_hash pending -> compute and re-verify facts at fact_recheck.
kill_conditions: Cannot source the build credits, the JSTOR/MIT facts, the charges and counts, the plea posture, and the aftermath to primary/official records plus reputable reporting; or cannot maintain the safe-handling rules (death once, no method, no single-cause) and the 35-years qualification.
